title: Paid upfront, item never ships
narrative: A buyer finds a sealed booster box listed well below market value and the seller insists on full payment before shipping, promising a tracking number 'tomorrow.' After payment via Zelle or friends-and-family, the tracking never arrives, the seller stops replying, and the listing or account vanishes. The buyer has no recourse because the payment method offered no protection.
redFlags: price far below market
seller demands full payment before shipping
vague promises of tracking later
wants friends-and-family or Zelle payment
account disappears after payment
saferAlternative: Use a platform with buyer protection and a payment method (like PayPal Goods & Services) that lets you dispute non-delivery.

title: Seller uploads a fake or recycled tracking number
narrative: A seller marks an order as shipped and provides a tracking number, so the buyer waits patiently, but the tracking either never updates beyond 'label created' or shows delivery to a different city or a one-ounce envelope that couldn't contain the item. The seller uses the tracking to delay disputes past protection windows or to make the order look fulfilled. By the time the buyer realizes the package isn't theirs, the seller has been paid out.
redFlags: tracking stuck on 'label created'
tracking weight or destination doesn't match item
delivery scan to wrong city
seller cites tracking to deny refund
saferAlternative: Verify the tracking shows the correct destination and a realistic package weight, and open a case before the protection window closes if it looks wrong.

title: Buyer 'accidentally' overpays and asks for the difference back
narrative: A buyer sends what looks like more than the agreed price, then messages in a panic claiming they fat-fingered the amount and asks the seller to refund the difference quickly. The original payment is fake, reversed, or sent via a method that can be clawed back, so once the seller wires back 'the difference' in real money they are out that cash plus the item. The urgency is engineered to make the seller act before verifying the funds truly cleared.
redFlags: buyer sends more than asking price
asks you to refund the difference
urgent pressure to send money back fast
payment can be reversed or is unverified
saferAlternative: Never refund an overpayment; instead refund the entire transaction through the same channel only after funds have irreversibly cleared.

title: Overpayment to cover a 'shipping agent' for the buyer
narrative: A buyer agrees to a high price sight-unseen and says they'll send extra to cover their own freight forwarder or shipping agent, asking the seller to forward that surplus to a third party. The inflated payment bounces or is reversed after the seller has already paid the bogus 'agent,' draining the seller's funds. The story exists only to route real money to a scammer's accomplice.
redFlags: buyer overpays to cover a third-party shipper
asks you to forward money to an agent
buyer too eager to pay full price sight-unseen
payment later reversed
saferAlternative: Only accept payment for the item itself and let the buyer arrange and pay their own shipper directly.

title: Fake escrow website holding the 'funds'
narrative: To build trust on a high-value graded card deal, one party proposes using an escrow service and sends a link to an official-looking site claiming the funds are now held safely until delivery. The site is a clone controlled by the scammer; the buyer's payment goes straight to the fraudster, or the seller ships believing escrow will release real money that never existed. Legitimate-sounding domain names and email confirmations make the fake service convincing.
redFlags: other party picks the escrow service and sends the link
unfamiliar escrow domain
escrow 'confirmation' emails with odd addresses
pressure to ship before funds verified independently
saferAlternative: Only use a well-known escrow provider you found and navigated to yourself, and confirm the holding independently of any link the counterparty sent.

title: Triangulation: your order shipped with a stolen credit card
narrative: A buyer purchases a sought-after card from a third-party seller and actually receives the item, shipped in a retailer's box, so everything seems fine. In reality the scammer used a stolen credit card to buy it from a legitimate store and pocketed the buyer's payment, leaving the buyer holding goods that may be flagged as fraudulent and the cardholder out the money. The deal feels legitimate precisely because a real product arrives.
redFlags: item arrives in a different retailer's packaging
seller has no real history but offers in-demand stock
price slightly below retail to move volume
invoice or packing slip names someone else
saferAlternative: Buy from authorized or established sellers and be wary of brand-new accounts moving popular in-stock items at suspiciously easy prices.

title: Buyer files a chargeback after receiving the item
narrative: A buyer receives a legitimate, accurately described card and then disputes the charge with their bank or PayPal claiming 'item not received' or 'not as described.' Because the seller shipped to the wrong address, lacked signature confirmation, or sold an item where condition is subjective, the chargeback succeeds and the buyer keeps both the card and the refund. This 'friendly fraud' weaponizes buyer-protection rules against honest sellers.
redFlags: buyer claims non-receipt despite delivery scan
no signature confirmation on a high-value item
buyer asks to ship to an address that doesn't match payment
vague 'not as described' claim with no photos
saferAlternative: Ship only to the verified payment address, require signature confirmation on high-value items, and keep timestamped photos and tracking as evidence.

title: Buyer claims the box arrived empty or with a swapped item
narrative: After a graded slab is delivered, the buyer opens a dispute saying the box was empty or contained a cheaper card, and demands a refund while keeping what was sent. Without recorded proof of packing and a matching weight on the shipping label, the seller struggles to win the case. Some buyers run this repeatedly across accounts on high-value collectibles.
redFlags: buyer says item was missing or swapped after delivery
request to return a different (lesser) card
buyer pressures for fast refund without returning item
no way to prove what was in the box
saferAlternative: Film the packing process unbroken, weigh the parcel, and use tamper-evident packaging plus serial-number photos for graded cards.

title: Spoofed 'you've been paid' email to rush shipping
narrative: A seller receives an email that looks like a PayPal payment notification saying the money is held pending shipment, and that they must ship and upload tracking before the funds release. The email is forged; no payment ever entered the seller's account, but the official-looking template and urgent instruction push the seller to ship for free. The 'pending until you provide tracking' framing is the giveaway.
redFlags: payment 'held' until you upload tracking
notification only by email, not in your account
sender address slightly off
pressure to ship before money shows in balance
saferAlternative: Confirm any payment by logging directly into your account, never trusting an email notification, before shipping anything.

title: Phishing link to 'confirm your account' steals login
narrative: A user gets a message that their listing was flagged or their payout is blocked, with a link to log in and 'verify' their account. The link leads to a near-perfect clone of the marketplace login that harvests the username, password, and two-factor code, letting the scammer take over the account, drain payouts, or post fraudulent listings under a trusted name. The message manufactures fear of losing access to make people click fast.
redFlags: unexpected 'account suspended/flagged' message
link to log in from the message itself
login page URL isn't the real domain
asks for your 2FA code on the page
saferAlternative: Ignore links in messages and navigate to the site yourself by typing the address, then check your account status there.

title: Fake 'update your payout info' message to a seller
narrative: A seller is told their bank details on file are outdated and payouts are paused until they re-enter banking information via a provided link. The form captures real bank or card details that the scammer uses for fraud, or redirects future payouts to the scammer's account. The message mimics routine platform housekeeping to lower suspicion.
redFlags: unsolicited request to re-enter banking info
link provided to a payout/settings page
claim that payouts are paused
urgency about missing a payout cycle
saferAlternative: Only update payment details from within your verified account dashboard, never from a link in an email or message.

title: Resealed or weighed booster packs and boxes
narrative: A buyer purchases 'sealed' booster packs or a booster box at a good price, but the packs were professionally resealed after the valuable cards were pulled, or the box was weighed to remove hit-heavy packs. The cellophane and crimp look convincing, so the buyer only discovers the loss after opening, by which point it's nearly impossible to prove. Loose packs and 'collection clear-out' lots are common vehicles.
redFlags: loose 'sealed' packs sold individually below market
weighed/searched lot language
reseal lines or off crimps on wrapper
seller discourages opening on camera
saferAlternative: Buy sealed product from authorized retailers, inspect wrapper crimps and weight, and avoid loose single packs from unknown sellers.

title: Counterfeit graded slab with a fake or reused cert
narrative: A buyer pays a premium for a high-grade slab, but the case is a counterfeit holder containing a fake or trimmed card, sometimes carrying a cert number cloned from a real, higher-value card. The label and font look right at a glance, but the certification number doesn't match the card when checked against the grader's database. Scammers rely on buyers trusting the slab without verifying.
redFlags: cert number doesn't match the grader's online lookup
label font/spacing slightly off
slab seams or case feel wrong
price below what that grade normally commands
saferAlternative: Verify the certification number on the grading company's official lookup and confirm the photo on file matches the exact card.

title: Proxy or fake single sold as a genuine card
narrative: A buyer arranges to buy a rare chase card after the seller moves the chat off-platform, and receives a high-quality counterfeit that fails the light test, lacks proper texture, or shows incorrect rosette printing under magnification. Because the deal happened over WhatsApp with an irreversible payment, there is no marketplace dispute available. Fake One Piece, Pokémon, and Magic singles have become very high quality.
redFlags: chat moved off-platform before sale
irreversible payment for a single card
card fails light/bend or texture test
seller avoids close-up authentication photos
saferAlternative: Buy expensive singles already graded, or transact on a platform with authentication and dispute resolution rather than over chat apps.

title: Google Voice OTP scam targeting a seller
narrative: An interested 'buyer' says they want to make sure the seller is a real person and not a bot, so they'll text a six-digit verification code that the seller should read back. That code is actually a Google Voice (or account) verification tied to the seller's phone number, and reading it back lets the scammer create accounts or hijack services in the seller's name. The request is framed as a harmless safety check.
redFlags: buyer wants to 'verify you're real' with a code
sends a six-digit code and asks you to read it back
verification before any normal sale steps
code arrives from Google/account services, not the buyer
saferAlternative: Never share any verification code you receive with anyone; legitimate buyers never need a code from your phone.

title: Scammer requests your 2FA code to 'complete the trade'
narrative: During a card trade, the other party claims a middleman bot or platform needs the user to confirm a security code sent to their phone or email to release the trade. The code is actually a password reset or login confirmation, and handing it over gives the scammer control of the account or a linked payment service. The 'bot needs it' story makes sharing the code feel procedural.
redFlags: asked to share a code to 'complete' a trade
claim a bot/middleman needs your code
code is actually a password reset or login
pressure to act before the code expires
saferAlternative: Treat every code as private; no legitimate trade, bot, or middleman ever needs a code sent to you.

title: Pressure to move the deal off-platform
narrative: Shortly after contact, the other party pushes to continue on WhatsApp, Telegram, or text and to pay by Zelle, Cash App, or crypto 'to skip the fees.' Moving off-platform strips away buyer/seller protections, dispute tools, and the paper trail, leaving the victim no recourse when the item or payment never materializes. The fee-saving pitch is the lure.
redFlags: wants to move chat to WhatsApp/Telegram
offers a discount to pay off-platform
insists on Zelle/Cash App/crypto to 'avoid fees'
reluctant to keep everything on the marketplace
saferAlternative: Keep all communication and payment on the original platform so its protections and dispute process still apply.

title: Seller insists on crypto-only payment
narrative: A seller of a high-value sealed case will only accept cryptocurrency or a wire transfer, citing chargeback fraud as the reason. Because crypto and wire payments are effectively irreversible and untraceable, the buyer has zero recovery options if the goods never arrive. The 'I've been burned by chargebacks' justification is used to disarm suspicion.
redFlags: crypto or wire transfer only
refuses all reversible payment methods
irreversible payment for a remote, sight-unseen item
blames chargebacks to justify it
saferAlternative: Insist on a payment method with buyer protection; walk away from any high-value remote deal that demands irreversible payment.

title: Asked to pay with gift cards
narrative: A seller asks the buyer to pay using retail gift cards and to send photos of the card numbers and PINs, framing it as quick and fee-free. Gift card codes are instantly drained and untraceable, so the buyer loses the money the moment the codes are shared and never receives the item. Any request for gift-card payment in a marketplace deal is effectively always a scam.
redFlags: asked to pay via gift cards
send photos of card numbers/PINs
gift cards framed as fast and fee-free
no legitimate reason to use gift cards for a private sale
saferAlternative: Never pay for marketplace goods with gift cards; treat any such request as a scam and stop the transaction.

title: Fake payment screenshot but no funds arrive
narrative: A buyer sends a screenshot showing a completed Zelle or Cash App payment and urges the seller to ship right away or hand over the item in person. The screenshot is edited or shows a 'pending' status that never settles, and the seller's account never actually receives the money. The image is meant to substitute for real confirmation.
redFlags: payment screenshot but no funds received
screenshot shows 'pending' or odd formatting
rush to ship/hand over before funds clear
buyer relies on the image as proof
saferAlternative: Only act after you independently see the funds settled in your own account, never on the strength of a screenshot.

title: Fake 'payment pending, upgrade to business' Zelle email
narrative: A scammer sends a spoofed email claiming a Zelle payment is on hold because the seller's account is 'personal' and needs to be upgraded to business, instructing the seller to send their own money to 'unlock' the larger payment. No payment exists; any money the seller sends to upgrade is simply stolen. The fake hold and upgrade fee are pure invention.
redFlags: email says payment is held pending an 'upgrade'
asked to send money to receive money
claim your account must become 'business'
all communication via email, nothing in your app
saferAlternative: Know that you never have to pay to receive a payment; verify everything inside your own banking app, not via email.

title: Bait-and-switch: photos show one card, a worse one arrives
narrative: A listing displays a pristine, high-grade card with sharp photos, but the package contains a different copy that is damaged, off-center, or a lower-value reprint. The seller counts on the buyer not bothering to dispute small differences or on subjective condition claims. Stock photos instead of the actual card are a frequent setup for this swap.
redFlags: listing uses stock photos, not the actual card
received card differs from photos in condition
seller cites subjective grading to deny it
no clear close-ups of the specific copy
saferAlternative: Require photos of the exact card you'll receive, including back and edges, and open a not-as-described case promptly if it differs.

title: Decoy shipment: heavy filler instead of the card
narrative: A buyer pays for an expensive slab and receives a package whose weight matches, but inside is a cheap decoy item or filler rather than the card pictured. The matching weight is engineered so the tracking and delivery look legitimate, defeating a naive 'item not received' claim. The buyer must escalate a not-as-described dispute with photos of what actually arrived.
redFlags: package weight matches but contents are wrong
received filler or a decoy item
delivery scan looks normal but item missing
seller insists tracking proves delivery
saferAlternative: Film opening the package on camera and immediately file a not-as-described dispute with photo/video evidence.

title: Shill bidding inflates an auction
narrative: A seller uses secondary accounts or accomplices to place bids on their own auction, manufacturing fake competition that drives the price up before the real buyer wins. The shill accounts often bid in tight increments late, retract suspiciously, or share patterns across the seller's listings. The buyer overpays for a card they could have gotten far cheaper.
redFlags: new zero-feedback accounts bidding repeatedly
bids jump in tiny increments late
same bidders across one seller's auctions
bid retractions just under the final price
saferAlternative: Set a firm maximum based on recent sold prices and don't chase an auction whose bidding pattern looks manufactured.

title: Private-listing shill bidding to hide accomplices
narrative: A seller runs a high-value card auction as a private listing so bidder IDs are hidden, then uses controlled accounts to push the price toward the genuine buyer's maximum. The privacy setting, sometimes justified as protecting buyer discretion, conveniently conceals the shill activity. Without visible bidder histories, the buyer can't spot the manipulation.
redFlags: high-value auction set to private bidders
price climbs steadily right to your max
seller justifies privacy unprompted
no way to inspect bidder history
saferAlternative: Be cautious with private high-value auctions and bid only your researched maximum, regardless of late competition.

title: Too-good-to-be-true bulk collection deal
narrative: A flashy account advertises an entire high-end collection or multiple sealed cases at a steep discount and shows stolen photos and 'proof of stock' clips. After collecting deposits or full payment from several buyers, the account blocks everyone and disappears. The professional-looking page and borrowed media create false legitimacy.
redFlags: entire collection or multiple cases at a steep discount
photos found elsewhere via reverse image search
wants a deposit to 'hold' the lot
account is new with bought-looking followers
saferAlternative: Reverse-image-search listing photos, demand a custom proof with a written note, and use a protected payment method.

title: Seller lured into accepting friends-and-family payment
narrative: A buyer asks the seller to accept PayPal friends-and-family to avoid fees, promising it's the same thing, then after delivery files a dispute or reverses the funding source. Friends-and-family payments waive seller protection and can still be clawed back through a funding-source reversal, so the seller loses the card and the money. The 'no fees' framing hides the loss of protection for both sides.
redFlags: buyer requests friends-and-family to skip fees
promises it's just as safe
high-value item via an unprotected payment
later funding-source reversal or bank dispute
saferAlternative: Always use Goods & Services for marketplace sales so seller protection applies, even though it costs a fee.

title: Impersonation of a well-known trusted seller
narrative: A scammer creates a look-alike account copying a reputable collector's or store's name, profile photo, and past post images, then DMs that account's followers with 'exclusive' deals. Buyers trust the familiar branding and pay, but the real seller has no knowledge of the order and the impostor vanishes. A nearly identical handle with an extra character or underscore is the tell.
redFlags: handle is a near-copy of a known seller's
unsolicited DM offering a special deal
reused photos from the real account
payment steered off-platform or to a new handle
saferAlternative: Confirm you're dealing with the genuine verified account and only buy through that seller's established, public channel.
